source_intake_record.raw_question_text: The figure 2.21 shows the cross section of an infinitely long circular cylinder of radius $3a$ with an infinitely long cylindrical hole of radius $a$ displaced so that its center is at a distance $a$ from the center of the big cylinder. The solid part of the cylinder carries a current $I$, distributed uniformly over the cross section, and out from the plane of the paper. Find the magnetic field at all points on the plane $P$ containing the axes of the cylinders.
source_intake_record.raw_answer_text: Inside the hole ($0<x<2a$): $H_y=\frac{I}{16\pi a}$; Inside the solid part ($2a\le x\le3a$ or $-3a\le x\le0$): $H_y=\frac{I(x^2-ax-a^2)}{16\pi a^2(x-a)}$; Outside the cylinder ($|x|>3a$): $H_y=\frac{(8x-9a)I}{16\pi x(x-a)}$
extraction_notes: image field contains a PIL image object representation rather than a file path or URL

multi_step: Solving requires combining multiple geometric conditions: big cylinder radius `3a`, hole radius `a`, and offset distance `a`.
The provided answer is piecewise over three spatial regions, indicating multi-case reasoning rather than a single direct lookup.
This is a magnetostatics derivation task, likely requiring superposition/comparison of fields from the full cylinder and removed hole.
verifiability: The answer is explicit and structured as piecewise formulas for `H_y` over stated `x` intervals, which supports rule-based checking.
`answer_present` is true and `registry_passed` is true, so the sample is registered cleanly enough for evaluation.

candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text: The figure 2.21 shows the cross section of an infinitely long circular cylinder of radius $3a$ with an infinitely long cylindrical hole of radius $a$ displaced so that its center is at a distance $a$ from the center of the big cylinder. The solid part of the cylinder carries a current $I$, distributed uniformly over the cross section, and out from the plane of the paper. Find the magnetic field at all points on the plane $P$ containing the axes of the cylinders.
candidate_problem_record.raw_answer_text: Inside the hole ($0<x<2a$): $H_y=\frac{I}{16\pi a}$; Inside the solid part ($2a\le x\le3a$ or $-3a\le x\le0$): $H_y=\frac{I(x^2-ax-a^2)}{16\pi a^2(x-a)}$; Outside the cylinder ($|x|>3a$): $H_y=\frac{(8x-9a)I}{16\pi x(x-a)}$

extraction_notes: image field contains a PIL image object representation rather than a file path or URL
metadata.reasoning_chain: According to the principle of superposition the field can be regarded as the difference of two fields $\mathbf{H}_{2}$ and $\mathbf{H}_{1}$, where $\mathbf{H}_{2}$ is the field produced by a solid (without the hole) cylinder of radius $3a$ and $\mathbf{H}_{1}$ is that produced by a cylinder of radius $a$ at the position of the hole. The current in each of these two cylinders is uniformly distributed over the cross section. The currents $I_{1}$ and $I_{2}$ in the small and large cylinders have current densities $-j$ and $+j$ respectively. Then as $I=I_{2}-I_{1}=9\pi a^{2}j-\pi a^{2}j=8\pi a^{2}j$, we have $j=\frac{I}{8\pi a^{2}}$ and $I_{1}=\pi a^{2}j=\frac{I}{8}$, $I_{2}=9\pi a^{2}j=\frac{9}{8}I$. Take the $z$-axis along the axis of the large cylinder with its positive direction in the direction of $I_{2}$, which we assume to be upwards from the plane of the paper. Take the $x$-axis crossing the axis of the small cylinder as shown in Fig. 2.21. Then the plane $P$ is the $xz$ plane, i.e., the plane $y=0$. Ampere's law gives $H_{1}$ and $H_{2}$ as follows (noting $r=\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}$, $r_{1}=\sqrt{(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}}$): for $r\le3a$, $H_{2x}=-\frac{Iy}{16\pi a^{2}}$, $H_{2y}=\frac{Ix}{16\pi a^{2}}$, and for $r>3a$, $H_{2x}=-\frac{9Iy}{16\pi(x^{2}+y^{2})}$, $H_{2y}=\frac{9Ix}{16\pi(x^{2}+y^{2})}$. Similarly, for $r_{1}\le a$, $H_{1x}=-\frac{Iy}{16\pi a^{2}}$, $H_{1y}=\frac{I(x-a)}{16\pi a^{2}}$, and for $r_{1}>a$, $H_{1x}=-\frac{Iy}{16\pi[(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}]}$, $H_{1y}=\frac{I(x-a)}{16\pi[(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}]}$. On the plane $P$, $H_{2x}=H_{1x}=0$, hence $H_{x}=0$, $H_{y}=H_{2y}-H_{1y}$. We therefore have: (1) Inside the hole ($0<x<2a$), $H_{y}=\frac{Ix}{16\pi a^{2}}-\frac{I(x-a)}{16\pi a^{2}}=\frac{I}{16\pi a}$. (2) Inside the solid part ($2a\le x\le3a$ or $-3a\le x\le0$), $H_{y}=\frac{Ix}{16\pi a^{2}}-\frac{I(x-a)}{16\pi[(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}]}=\frac{I(x^{2}-ax-a^{2})}{16\pi a^{2}(x-a)}$. (3) Outside the cylinder ($|x|>3a$), $H_{y}=\frac{9Ix}{16\pi(x^{2}+y^{2})}-\frac{I(x-a)}{16\pi[(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}]}=\frac{(8x-9a)I}{16\pi x(x-a)}$.

problem_main_record.reasoning_chain: According to the principle of superposition the field can be regarded as the difference of two fields $\mathbf{H}_{2}$ and $\mathbf{H}_{1}$, where $\mathbf{H}_{2}$ is the field produced by a solid (without the hole) cylinder of radius $3a$ and $\mathbf{H}_{1}$ is that produced by a cylinder of radius $a$ at the position of the hole. The current in each of these two cylinders is uniformly distributed over the cross section. The currents $I_{1}$ and $I_{2}$ in the small and large cylinders have current densities $-j$ and $+j$ respectively. Then as $I=I_{2}-I_{1}=9\pi a^{2}j-\pi a^{2}j=8\pi a^{2}j$, we have $j=\frac{I}{8\pi a^{2}}$ and $I_{1}=\pi a^{2}j=\frac{I}{8}$, $I_{2}=9\pi a^{2}j=\frac{9}{8}I$. Take the $z$-axis along the axis of the large cylinder with its positive direction in the direction of $I_{2}$, which we assume to be upwards from the plane of the paper. Take the $x$-axis crossing the axis of the small cylinder as shown in Fig. 2.21. Then the plane $P$ is the $xz$ plane, i.e., the plane $y=0$. Ampere's law gives $H_{1}$ and $H_{2}$ as follows (noting $r=\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}$, $r_{1}=\sqrt{(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}}$): for $r\le3a$, $H_{2x}=-\frac{Iy}{16\pi a^{2}}$, $H_{2y}=\frac{Ix}{16\pi a^{2}}$, and for $r>3a$, $H_{2x}=-\frac{9Iy}{16\pi(x^{2}+y^{2})}$, $H_{2y}=\frac{9Ix}{16\pi(x^{2}+y^{2})}$. Similarly, for $r_{1}\le a$, $H_{1x}=-\frac{Iy}{16\pi a^{2}}$, $H_{1y}=\frac{I(x-a)}{16\pi a^{2}}$, and for $r_{1}>a$, $H_{1x}=-\frac{Iy}{16\pi[(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}]}$, $H_{1y}=\frac{I(x-a)}{16\pi[(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}]}$. On the plane $P$, $H_{2x}=H_{1x}=0$, hence $H_{x}=0$, $H_{y}=H_{2y}-H_{1y}$. We therefore have: (1) Inside the hole ($0<x<2a$), $H_{y}=\frac{Ix}{16\pi a^{2}}-\frac{I(x-a)}{16\pi a^{2}}=\frac{I}{16\pi a}$. (2) Inside the solid part ($2a\le x\le3a$ or $-3a\le x\le0$), $H_{y}=\frac{Ix}{16\pi a^{2}}-\frac{I(x-a)}{16\pi[(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}]}=\frac{I(x^{2}-ax-a^{2})}{16\pi a^{2}(x-a)}$. (3) Outside the cylinder ($|x|>3a$), $H_{y}=\frac{9Ix}{16\pi(x^{2}+y^{2})}-\frac{I(x-a)}{16\pi[(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}]}=\frac{(8x-9a)I}{16\pi x(x-a)}$.

field_audit_records.before_value: Inside the hole ($0<x<2a$): $H_y=\frac{I}{16\pi a}$; Inside the solid part ($2a\le x\le3a$ or $-3a\le x\le0$): $H_y=\frac{I(x^2-ax-a^2)}{16\pi a^2(x-a)}$; Outside the cylinder ($|x|>3a$): $H_y=\frac{(8x-9a)I}{16\pi x(x-a)}$
field_audit_records.after_value: Inside the hole ($0<x<2a$): $H_y=\frac{I}{16\pi a}$; Inside the solid part ($2a\le x\le3a$ or $-3a\le x\le0$): $H_y=\frac{I(x^2-ax-a^2)}{16\pi a^2(x-a)}$; Outside the cylinder ($|x|>3a$): $H_y=\frac{(8x-9a)I}{16\pi x(x-a)}$

variants.title: SeePhys 开放题
variants.rewritten_question_text: The figure 2.21 shows the cross section of an infinitely long circular cylinder of radius $3a$ with an infinitely long cylindrical hole of radius $a$ displaced so that its center is at a distance $a$ from the center of the big cylinder. The solid part of the cylinder carries a current $I$, distributed uniformly over the cross section, and out from the plane of the paper. Find the magnetic field at all points on the plane $P$ containing the axes of the cylinders.
variants.expected_answer: Inside the hole ($0<x<2a$): $H_y=\frac{I}{16\pi a}$; Inside the solid part ($2a\le x\le3a$ or $-3a\le x\le0$): $H_y=\frac{I(x^2-ax-a^2)}{16\pi a^2(x-a)}$; Outside the cylinder ($|x|>3a$): $H_y=\frac{(8x-9a)I}{16\pi x(x-a)}$
